data.title: Workday: A Competitive Analysis With Oracle And SAP
data.description:
Investment thesis

Workday, Inc. (WDAY), is one of three leading competitors providing HCM (human capital management) and ERP (enterprise resource planning). Along with SAP SE (SAP) and Oracle Corporation (ORCL), it helps other firms do full-cycle human resource management and unify core business functions.

In this analysis, we compare the three companies across multiple financial metrics and conclude with a Buy rating for Workday. But, we also highlight the growth potential of one of the other two.

About Workday

The company offers enterprise cloud solutions powered by artificial intelligence [AI] to help companies "maintain accounting information; manage financial processes, such as payables and receivables; identify real-time financial, operational, and management insights; perform financial consolidation; reduce time-to-close; promote internal control and auditability; and achieve consistency across finance operations." (Source: Seeking Alpha).

SAP SE and Oracle Corporation provide similar but not identical offerings to their customers. For example, all three offer HCM and ERP services, but with different emphases.

The payroll consultancy firm Leppington Group offered this summary of the three firms:

"Oracle is ideal for large, global enterprises with complex payroll needs. SAP SuccessFactors is well-suited for organisations already using SAP ERP systems and requiring strong global capabilities. Workday is best for medium to large enterprises looking for a modern, user-friendly, and scalable solution."

And under the headline "Which Software Should You Buy in 2025", an article in AppCloneScript summarizes,

"Select Workday HCM if you want a holistic, intuitive, and contemporary HR software with inbuilt analytics and fast delivery. It's the preferred solution for customers in search of a proven technology foundation for the digital transformation of their business, as well as HCM professionals in organizations of any size who need to be part of a connected HCM experience.

"Go with SAP SuccessFactors if you're a large company with worldwide complexity, and especially if you're already invested in the SAP ecosystem. Its global payroll service and learning management are top of the market.

"Opt for Oracle HCM Cloud if you want a high level of configurability as well as robust analytics and integration with other Oracle apps. It applies to complicated business structures and hybrid clouds."

For investors who want exposure to HCM and/or ERP stocks, these three offer different approaches. Is one a better investment than the others? That is a question we will try to answer by analyzing their fundamentals.

Revenue, net income, and earnings

The following five-year tables show average year-over-year and forward growth rates for revenue and diluted EPS (from the growth tables at Seeking Alpha):

Workday's diluted EPS growth over the past five years is unavailable because it had a loss in 2023. That was followed by an unusually large gain in 2024.

What comes through is that Workday is a tech growth stock, while Oracle and SAP appear to be mature companies with only modest gains expected in coming years. That's reinforced by share repurchases and the payment of dividends by Oracle and SAP, while Workday continues to invest in growth (but does buy back shares). Investors interested in capital gains will be better off with Workday, while income investors should look at the other two companies (their dividend yields are comparable).

Margins and cash flow

When it comes to net margins, Oracle is clearly the leader, while Workday is the laggard, and SAP falls between them:

Surprisingly, the numbers are reversed for free cash flow per share, with Workday ahead of SAP and far ahead of Oracle:

Why has Oracle's FCF declined so much? A scan of its cash flow statement shows a huge increase in its net capital expenditures. It has gone from $6.87 billion in the fiscal year that ended in May 2024 to $21.22 billion in fiscal 2025 and now stands at $48.25 billion on a TTM basis.

At the same time, it is also loading up on long-term debt. That has risen from $76.26 billion in fiscal 2024 to $124.72 billion on a TTM basis. In both the capex and debt cases, it has invested heavily in AI research and development.

Comparing the three firms, Workday has an edge-for now, at least. Its FCF per share means it has more capital available for acquisitions and organic growth, leading to higher returns for shareholders. However, if Oracle's big investments pay off, it could become the undisputed leader in terms of margins and cash flow.

Returns on total capital

Return on total capital provides an overview of what shareholders are getting out of their capital contributions and the debt taken on by management:

Two contrasting scenarios have unfolded over the past several years. First, Workday and SAP are increasing their returns, and second, Oracle has been sliding down.

For Workday and SAP, artificial intelligence is driving new opportunities, as their customers seek to reduce costs, offset a shrinking workforce, and more. New technologies to achieve these challenges are emerging, and their customers want to take advantage of them. If they do not, they may suffer competitive disadvantages.

Oracle, too, has enjoyed growth for the same reason. And, as we saw, it has pushed up its capex and debt even more.

SAP is the best performer here, with Workday not far behind. Oracle is heading in the wrong direction-for now. Again, we point to Oracle's potential.

Valuations

Of course, investors will want to compare the valuations to determine how much it will cost them for one dollar of earnings. The following table provides two conventional measures and two PEG valuations, the latter to capture the value of earnings growth:

Note the very low PEG TTM ratio for SAP; that reflects the firm's rapid earnings growth in the past year. In Q4-2024, it earned $2.78 per share, and by Q4-2025, it had grown EPS to $7.22 per share, which is extraordinary growth. Since PEG FWD is more than 10 times higher, analysts expect that rate of growth to get back to normal going forward.

Workday's high PEG TTM ratio reflects the opposite of SAP's, with slow growth in the past year, from $1.98 to $2.61. However, its PEG FWD is much lower and better, with analysts expecting stronger growth this year.

All three stocks are better values now than they were in the second half of 2025, as their share prices slid, almost in unison:

EPS forecasts

Let's now look at the specific EPS estimates provided by Wall Street analysts (note the variations with the growth table figures above):

A couple of notes: first, the three firms have different year-ending dates, hence the Year 1, etc. Second, Workday is covered by 41 analysts, SAP by 8, and Oracle by 37. All three have fewer analysts as the forecasts go out further.

What do the results suggest? Earlier in this article, it appeared Workday was the growth stock to beat. Now, and with these data points, the growth lead may actually belong to Oracle. Its average annual EPS growth is expected to be roughly 50% higher than those of SAP and Workday, as its heavy capital and debt investments kick in and raise its fundamentals. Curiously, though, the analysts expect growth of only 7.00% in what I'm calling Year 2.

As we will see next, the companies have mostly beaten estimates in the past year.

Quality of earnings

Workday has consistently beaten estimates over the past four quarters. In the most recent quarter, analysts expected revenue of $2.52 billion, while the company delivered $2.53 billion. Similarly, on EPS, analysts forecast $2.32 per share, while the actual number was $2.47.

Oracle beat both revenue and earnings estimates in its latest quarter, Q3-fiscal-year-2026. In Q2, it beat on earnings and missed on revenue. Q1 saw double misses, while Q4-2025 produced beats on both revenue and EPS.

For SAP, Q4-2025 earnings topped estimates, while it missed on revenue. It was the same mix for Q3, Q2, and Q1.

Give Workday a slight lead in this category, with its consistent overperformance, a performance that should help maintain analysts' optimism about future results.

"Should" is an appropriate verb here. Of the 40 Workday revisions posted by Wall Street analysts, 10 are ups and 30 are downs. SAP, on the other hand, has six Ups and one down. Oracle has 34 ups and three downs. Analysts have far greater confidence in Oracle than in Workday and SAP, it seems.

Despite the revision numbers above, when it comes to ratings, Workday has 19 Strong Buys, seven Buys and 17 Holds.

For SAP, the analysts have nine Strong Buys, four Buys, and three Holds.

Oracle comes in with the best ratings: 28 Strong Buys, seven Buys, eight Holds, and one Strong Sell. Again, the edge goes to Oracle.

Turning to returns on total capital [ROTC], SAP has taken the lead from Oracle, while Workday lags them both:

Momentum

With the share price of all three firms declining since last October, there is no positive momentum.

Given that all fell about the same amount over about the same period, we should look to industry headwinds. That includes an increase in interest rates last fall, and increases in the cost of borrowing likely reduced demand among their customers.

The shareholder exit began about the same time as Oracle started boosting its capex and debt. Specifically, in the quarter that ended on November 30, 2025, Oracle boosted its capex by nearly 50%, from $8.50 billion to $12.03 billion. And in the fourth quarter, it upped its capex again by over 50%, from $12.03 billion to $18.64 billion. Investors in SAP and Workday likely asked themselves if their companies would need to make similar commitments.

And, there was other news that kept pressure on prices. In its Q4 fiscal 2026 first quarter earnings report, Workday reported subscription sales had slipped, albeit modestly.

Investors likely were concerned, too, by the increasing power of AI and what it might mean for HCM and ERP workflows. Would AI providers eat into existing business relationships? Could there be major disruptions? Would it drive down prices or margins?

The three firms are integrating AI into their processes and systems, but would that be enough to maintain their competitive edges?

Ratings

Despite these concerns, I believe the outlook for all three stocks is positive. They have well established positions in their industry, with most of big business as their customers. Incumbency is not a moat, but it is a solid advantage and provides time to adapt to the innovations of other firms.

As we saw in the EPS estimates, the analysts expect earnings to grow through at least the next three years. For Workday and SAP, we're looking at average expected returns of 14% to 15%, while Oracle is expected to post average returns of nearly 22%.

Looking specifically at Workday. I give it a Buy rating, based on its history of growing ROTC and beating estimates. And while investors may be concerned about HCM and ERP at the company, CEO Aneel Bhusri said on the Q4-2026 earnings call,

"You've all heard the narrative out there that HR and ERP will be replaced or relegated to the background by AI. I personally just don't see that happening. Our application domains are really, really hard to build. I've been working in the HR and ERP space for over 30 years. These are true systems of record that must process transactions with absolute accuracy and speed, enforce complex security models and comply with statutory and regulatory requirements all over the world. That kind of complexity is very hard to replicate. No amount of vibe coding is going to produce an HR or an ERP system."

Investors interested in Workday might also take a closer look at Oracle, which is expecting faster EPS growth because of its major capex and debt investments.

Interestingly, both Workday and Oracle are expected to keep growing their bottom lines, while their share prices show no sign of recovery. If this divergence continues to expand, there may be value investing opportunities in the near future.

Risk factors

The Seeking Alpha system has placed a warning label across the Workday summary page, warning the firm is at high risk of performing badly. It points specifically to decelerating momentum and negative EPS revisions. In my previous analyses of these warnings, I have found they depend too much on past and current data, and do not factor in forward potential. Nevertheless, investors should consider the warning, and investors considering Oracle and SAP should do the same.

The CEO of Workday considers the threat of AI disruption relatively low, but since we are in the early stages of AI development, investors should consider this threat. No one yet knows where the limits, or even the boundaries, lie.

In a highly competitive tech industry, it is essential to keep up with the leaders and even take the lead at least part of the time. All three companies face this challenge, but it may be more of a burden for Workday, which is smaller than its two rivals by market caps.

Securing customers data, which in the HCM and ERP areas is highly sensitive, is also essential. Any breach of data could lead to reputational and/or financial harm.

All three firms operate internationally, which brings in new exposures and risks. Compliance with regulations and legislation becomes more difficult with each new country they enter. From another perspective, foreign expansion also produces currency exchange risks.

Conclusion

I have confidence Workday will continue to deliver capital gains to its shareholders, along with modest gains through share buybacks. That is the main reason for my Buy rating. Based on the EPS estimates, I expect much the same from SAP, although shareholders in it will receive dividends as well.

Oracle, however, may have the greatest growth potential. The EPS estimates indicate strong capital gains as its investments show up in higher revenue and earnings.

data.title: Molina Healthcare: A Cyclical Long-Term Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Molina Healthcare (MOH) seems misunderstood after a temporary disruption in earnings, making now an attractive entry point for long-term investors. The severe Q4 underperformance appears to be driven by both Medicaid managed care's cyclical nature and retroactive rate adjustments, rather than fundamental problems in the business model.

In the past, Medicaid managed care has operated within a predictable cycle of cost increases that exceed state reimbursement rates. These ultimately return to normal after rates are adjusted. Therefore, it is likely that some form of adjustment in California rates will appear to help reduce some of the burden on Molina's profit margin. Marketplace prices have also increased since the start of 2026, and the Medicare Advantage Part D (MAPD) segment will be exited by 2027, further suggesting that the current downturn is temporary.

Lastly, the current outlook of $5.00 in EPS for 2026 does not seem to reflect normalized earnings potential. This includes ~$2.50 in one-time costs associated with ramping up in Florida and the exit of the Medicare Advantage business. If we were to remove these estimated costs, it is likely that Molina could earn ~$7.50 in normalized EPS. Furthermore, management has indicated that their "embedded earnings" model estimates the company's EPS could reach over $11 in the long term. This is based upon its growth pipeline of awarded government-backed contracts transitioning from investment phase to profitability in the next 12-24 months.

Given these developments, I believe current estimates aren't fully taking into account the recovery potential, contract maturation, or the removal of margin headwinds. This leads me to rate the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60, where I expect this recovery to boost estimates as results materialize.

The Q4 Miss

The market's response to Q4 2025 was severe, with the stock dropping ~25% the day after reporting an adjusted EPS of -$2.75, down from $5.05 YoY. Molina noted that its Q4 results were weighed down by around $2.00 per share of unfavorable premium retroactive adjustments in its California Medicaid business. These are revenue items where the state retrospectively adjusted reimbursement, which is a common feature of Medicaid managed care that can often create earnings surprises. The Medicaid MCR for Q4 printed at 93.5%, elevated by cost pressure and timing mismatches, and the Medicare MCR hit 97.5%, driven by high-acuity utilization and the weak MAPD product. The Marketplace MCR was elevated at 92.5%.

With California retroactive adjustments reversing over time as rates catch up, it suggests that these pressures are temporary. The MAPD product, the single biggest negative Medicare item, is exiting entirely for 2027. Marketplace MCR is expected to normalize as rate increases averaging ~30% in 2026 work their way through the book. This is a common pattern in the Medicaid industry margin cycle, and with Molina's stock heavily impacted by these short-term headwinds, it creates an opportunity to buy at the trough as the industry normalizes.

Medicaid Rate Restoration

The managed care Medicaid space moves through a multiyear cycle of growth and decline. Each year the states adjust capitation rates based on predictions of what the medical services will cost. As long as the medical services provided are higher than the actuarially predicted costs (as was the case from 2024 to 2025), the managed care providers take the losses and lobby states for rate corrections. Once the state agrees to an increase (usually within 1-2 years after the initial loss), it allows the providers to make up for their losses by increasing their margins. This has happened multiple times with periods such as 2008-2010 (as unemployment rose), 2015-2017 (millions of new members due to the ACA), and currently 2024-2026. There's evidence that we are now at the trough, with Molina's CEO stating:

We believe our 2026 forecast for Medicaid is the trough for managed Medicaid margins. In this margin trough, we expect that Molina Medicaid will produce a low single-digit margin, not losses, and that the market is underfunded by 300 to 400 basis points.

Every time this has occurred, the managed care companies swung to profit and brought strong per-share gains with them within the next 12-24 months.

The $2.50 EPS Burden

The $5.00 in 2026 adjusted EPS guidance includes $2.50 in burdens explicitly stated by management. The first is a non-recurring $1.50 EPS burden related to the start-up costs associated with the new Florida CMS Medicaid contract. While it will take some time before the contract is profitable, it is estimated that the Florida contract will generate $6 billion in premium revenue yearly once operational. While it does pose short-term headwinds, it makes the long-term story much more rewarding now that those headwinds appear to have been priced in by the market.

The second element is a $1.00 EPS burden, which represents the underperformance of MAPD, which is being discontinued by 2027. At its current scale and composition of members, this product is structurally unprofitable. In 2027, this $1.00 EPS headwind will be $0, placing the company in a better position for long-term growth. When adding these two elements back to guidance, the normalized business is generating ~$7.50 in EPS for 2026. This estimate places them closer to past performance of $11.03 adjusted EPS in 2025, despite having a more unfavorable environment. With a more than 50% drop over the last year, the market appears to have overestimated the short-term headwinds given a likely return to strong earnings.

The $11+ Embedded Earnings

Management said that as a result of newly embedded earnings, the company now expects to earn more than $11 EPS in future years. This indicates the earnings power those contracts could contribute once they reach target margins. One contract alone, the Florida CMS contract, includes a $6 billion premium annually at Molina's historical Medicaid profit margin. These are not speculative contracts and have already been funded by governmental agencies. As the contracts mature over the following years, they move from the embedded earnings to tangible results. When combined with the restored rates on the rest of the current Medicaid book and removal of MAPD, the FY 2027 EPS comes into focus. With estimates of $8.20 EPS and a stock price at ~$150 per share, Molina is trading at about 18x FY 2027 earnings. This appears quite cheap for a managed care company at the beginning of a potential rate restoration cycle.

Upcoming Earnings

Molina reports its Q1 2026 earnings on April 22, 2026, with adjusted EPS estimates of $1.94. This number represents an expected decline from $6.08 YoY, which is already included in guidance. While Q1 has historically been the weakest season, Q1 2026 also absorbs the start-up costs associated with the Florida CMS contract and MAPD drag. Instead of looking at EPS, I'll be looking at other metrics to confirm rate progress.

The first is Medicaid MCR. The company's guidance calls for a rate of 92.9% in 2026, which is elevated over historical norms of 88-89%. A Q1 Medicaid MCR below 93.5%, combined with comments indicating cost trends are stabilizing or moderating, would represent the first signal that the trough is forming. On the other hand, a Q1 Medicaid MCR above 94% would be a negative indicator that the rate-cost imbalance has worsened prior to improving.

Any language regarding Medicaid rate restoration is also another thing to look out for. Specifically, management stated that the broad Medicaid market requires rates to exceed trend by 200-300 basis points to achieve program equilibrium. Any commentary on state rate filings, particularly California, Texas, and Ohio (Molina’s largest markets), would help determine how quickly the trough converts to recovery.

Finally, I will be watching the Florida contract ramp progress. Any update on member enrollment pace, operational build-out, or claims experience in Florida would help investors assess when the burden turns into contribution. Overall, positive developments on these fronts (which I expect) could help increase the potential for a re-rating of the stock's valuation as the market realizes the recovery is beginning.

Valuation

A price per share of ~$150 puts Molina at about 30x FY 2026 forecasted EPS. That seems a little pricey until we remove the temporary burden of $2.50 from our 2026 forecasted EPS. After removing this, the company would be trading at 20x normalized 2026 EPS. By FY 2027, the consensus estimate for EPS is $8.20, putting the stock at ~18x FY 2027 EPS currently. For my 12-month price target, I'm applying a 23x multiple, bringing the price per share to $188.60. This reflects a premium as Molina's story changes from a troubled provider to a diversified growth story, powered by interest rate normalization, the exit of MAPD losses, and the ramp-up of the Florida contracts.

Risks

The greatest risk is how long it will take for states to normalize Medicaid rates. If the major states are unable to do so in the coming years, then EPS would be negatively impacted and affect earnings.

Florida contract execution is the second risk. This is the largest contract in Molina's pipeline and represents $6 billion in annual premium with the state. Implementation costs of this contract also put an additional $1.50 EPS burden on the company. As a result, if the Florida startup slows down, guidance may miss expectations.

Conclusion

The response to Molina's earnings reset has caused the company to be valued based on short-term depressed profitability instead of its sustainable earnings potential. With better earnings visibility because of normalizing Medicaid rates, scaling in Florida, and the exit of Medicare Advantage losses by 2027, there will likely be greater clarity for earnings through 2027. This provides support for re-expansion of multiples, which is why I'm rating the stock a Buy with a 12-month price target of $188.60.
